audience: security practitioners, red teamers, blue teamers
core_claim: HexStrike AI is an open-source (MIT) MCP framework that lets AI agents autonomously orchestrate 150+ security tools; shortly after its 2025 release, Check Point Research reported threat actors discussing it to speed up exploitation of fresh Citrix NetScaler zero-days — collapsing the disclosure-to-exploitation window from weeks to hours, though direct attribution remains forum chatter, not confirmed forensics.

caption:
An open-source tool just let an AI agent drive 150+ hacking tools by itself, and within hours of a fresh Citrix zero-day, attackers were already talking about pointing it at the internet.

That tool is HexStrike AI (v6.0). MIT-licensed, built for authorized red teams and bug bounty work: you hand an AI agent like Claude or GPT a plain-language goal, and a decision engine picks the tools, chains recon into exploitation, and retries what fails. Genuinely useful for defenders testing their own systems.

Here's the part that matters. After Citrix disclosed three NetScaler zero-days last year, Check Point Research saw threat actors on underground forums discussing HexStrike to scan and exploit them. One bug, CVE-2025-7775, scored 9.2 and had roughly 28,000 boxes exposed. Check Point estimated AI could compress exploit development from days or weeks to under ten minutes.

The honest caveat: that's forum chatter, not confirmed forensic attribution. No fake panic. But the direction is real. The gap between a disclosure and mass exploitation is collapsing.

So the takeaway isn't 'AI hacks everything.' It's that patch velocity and exposure management matter more than ever, because you get less time to apply the same fundamentals. Patch internet-facing gear first, watch threat intel on disclosure day, and tune detection for that window.

How fast can your team realistically patch an internet-facing zero-day?

Save this for your next patch-SLA review. Follow for AI security without the fake panic.
comment_prompt: Honestly, what's your real patch SLA for an internet-facing zero-day: hours, days, or weeks?
alt_text: Slide 1 (cover): a robotic arm grabbing tools off a huge wall in a blur of motion; headline reads that AI just cut exploit development from weeks to minutes.
Slide 2 (context): a robotic arm poised before a wall of mechanical tools with one control thread from above; text says an open-source framework lets an AI agent run 150+ security tools.
Slide 3 (what happened): a crack in a dark wall with a swarm of small drones streaming through; text says hours after a Citrix zero-day dropped, attackers discussed using it to mass-exploit.
Slide 4 (how it works): a branching river of light splitting into self-correcting channels; text says a decision engine turns a plain-language goal into a recon-to-exploit loop.

Slide 5 (shrinking window): an hourglass with sand rushing through too fast; text says the gap between disclosure and mass exploitation is now hours, not weeks, with ~28,000 NetScaler boxes exposed to CVE-2025-7775.
Slide 6 (defense): a figure rapidly bolting a steel plate over a gap in a wall; text says patch internet-facing gear first and fast, and assume n-day exploitation is automated.
Slide 7 (takeaway): a steep staircase flattening into a gentle ramp; text says AI lowers the skill floor for attackers, so patch velocity matters more, not less.
Slide 8 (CTA): a stopwatch held up with a finger on the button; text asks how fast your team can patch an internet-facing zero-day.

sources.supports: Open-source (MIT) MCP server letting AI agents autonomously run 150+ security tools and 12+ agents for authorized pentest/bug-bounty/red-team use; includes explicit authorized-use-only disclaimer.

sources.source: Kali Linux — HexStrike AI tool page
sources.supports: Official packaging as an AI-powered MCP cybersecurity automation platform with 150+ modules and an adaptive AI decision engine; distributed in Kali.

sources.source: Cyber Security News — HexStrike AI red-team tool (v6.0)
sources.supports: v6.0 MCP-based framework with a FastMCP orchestration brain / Intelligent Decision Engine connecting LLMs to offensive tooling; six AI client integrations.

sources.source: Check Point Research — Hexstrike-AI: When LLMs Meet Zero-Day Exploitation
sources.supports: Threat actors on underground forums discussed using HexStrike to exploit Citrix NetScaler zero-days within ~12 hours of disclosure; AI could cut exploit development from days/weeks to under 10 minutes; explicit caveat that direct involvement is chatter, not confirmed forensic attribution.

sources.source: The Hacker News — Threat Actors Weaponize HexStrike AI to Exploit Citrix Flaws
sources.supports: Independent reporting on the Check Point findings; Citrix NetScaler CVE-2025-7775 (CVSS 9.2, unauth RCE) and ~28,000 exposed endpoints (ShadowServer).

narration.text: An open-source AI tool can now run over a hundred and fifty hacking tools on its own.
Hours after a Citrix zero-day dropped, attackers were already discussing using it to mass-exploit.
So patch your internet-facing systems first, and fast. Assume exploitation is automated now.
AI lowers the skill floor for attackers. Patch speed matters more, not less.
How fast can your team patch an internet-facing zero-day?
